1737	\begin{lemma}
1738	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1739	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1740	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1741	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1742	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1743	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1744	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1745	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1746	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1747	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1748	  functor
1749	  \[
1750	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1751	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1752	  \]
1753	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1754	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1755	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1756	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1757	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1758	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1759	  canonical isomorphism
1760	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1761	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1762	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1763	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1764	\end{lemma}
1765	
1766	\begin{proof}
1767	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1768	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1769	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1770	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1771	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1772	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1773	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1774	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1775	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1776	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1777	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1778	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1779	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1780	  same cover.
1781	\end{proof}

1783	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1784	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1785	
1786	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1787	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1788	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1789	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1790	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1791	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1792	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1793	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1794	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1795	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1796	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1797	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1798	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1799	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1801	\begin{definition}\leanok
1802	[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
1803	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
1804	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
1805	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1806	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
1807	  % modules), Definition 01CS (definition-invertible) + Lemma 0B8K
1808	  % (lemma-invertible, the "$\exists\,\mathcal{N}$" characterisation)
1809	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4079).
1810	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1811	  % "\begin{definition}
1812	  % \label{definition-invertible}
1813	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
1814	  % {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
1815	  % of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
1816	  % the functor
1817	  % $$
1818	  % \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
1819	  % \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
1820	  % $$
1821	  % is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
1822	  % {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1823	  % to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
1824	  % \end{definition}
1825	  % [\dots]
1826	  % \begin{lemma}
1827	  % \label{lemma-invertible}
1828	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
1829	  % be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
1830	  % \begin{enumerate}
1831	  % \item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
1832	  % \item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
1833	  % such that
1834	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
1835	  % \end{enumerate}
1836	  % In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
1837	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
1838	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
1839	  % \end{lemma}"
1840	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1841	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
1842	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1843	  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
1844	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
1845	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
1846	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
1847	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1848	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1849	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1850	  \[
1851	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1852	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1853	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1854	  \]
1855	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
1856	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1857	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1858	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1859	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1860	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1861	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1862	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1863	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1864	  \(\otimes_X\).
1865	\end{definition}

1867	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
1868	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
1869	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}

1916	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1917	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1918	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1919	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1920	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1921	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1922	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1923	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1924	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1925	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1926	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1927	  01CR).
1928	
1929	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1930	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1931	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1932	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1933	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1934	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1935	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1936	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1937	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1938	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1939	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1940	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1941	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1942	
1943	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1944	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1945	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1946	  \[
1947	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1948	    \quad
1949	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1950	    \quad
1951	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1952	    \quad
1953	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1954	  \]
1955	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1956	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1957	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1958	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1959	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1960	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1961	
1962	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1963	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1964	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1965	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1966	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1967	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1968	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1969	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1970	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1971	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1972	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1973	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1974	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1975	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1976	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1977	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1978	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1979	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1980	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1981	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1982	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1983	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1984	  pinned commit.
1985	
1986	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1987	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1988	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1989	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1990	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1991	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1992	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1993	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1994	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1995	
1996	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1997	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1998	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1999	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2000	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2001	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2002	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2003	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2004	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2005	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2006	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2007	  already supplies.
2008	\end{lemma}

2073	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2074	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.

2076	\begin{theorem}
2077	
2078	\leanok
2079	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2080	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2081	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2082	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2083	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2084	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2085	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2086	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2087	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2088	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2089	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2090	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2091	
2092	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2093	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2094	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2095	  % groups)
2096	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2097	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2098	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2099	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2100	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2101	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2102	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2103	  % \end{dfn}
2104	  %  [\dots]
2105	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2106	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2107	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2108	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2109	  % topologies  by
2110	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2111	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2112	  %  \end{dfn}"
2113	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2114	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2115	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2116	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2117	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2118	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2119	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2120	  \[
2121	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2122	  \]
2123	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2124	  abelian-group structure with addition
2125	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2126	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2127	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2128	  inverse
2129	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2130	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2131	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2132	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2133	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2134	  map
2135	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2136	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2137	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2138	\end{theorem}

2140	\begin{proof}
2141	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2142	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2143	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2144	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2145	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2146	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2147	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2148	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2149	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2150	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2151	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2152	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2153	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2154	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2155	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2156	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2157	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2158	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2159	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2160	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2161	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2162	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2163	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2164	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2165	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2166	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2167	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2168	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2169	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2170	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2171	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2172	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2173	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2174	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2175	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2176	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2177	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2178	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2179	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2180	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2181	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2182	
2183	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2184	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2185	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2186	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2187	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2188	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2189	
2190	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2191	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2192	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2193	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2194	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2195	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2196	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2197	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2198	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2199	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2200	\end{proof}

2344	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
2345	  infrastructure)}
2346	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
2347	
2348	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
2349	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
2350	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
2351	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
2352	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
2353	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
2354	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
2355	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
2356	no such
2357	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
2358	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
2359	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
2360	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
2361	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
2362	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
2363	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
2364	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
2365	
2366	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
2367	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
2368	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
2369	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
2370	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
2371	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
2372	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
2373	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
2374	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
2375	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
2376	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
2377	
2378	\begin{definition}
2379	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
2380	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2381	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
2382	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2383	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2384	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2385	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
2386	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2387	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2388	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2389	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2390	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2391	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2392	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2393	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2394	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2395	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
2396	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
2397	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
2398	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
2399	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2400	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
2401	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
2402	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
2403	  \[
2404	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
2405	  \]
2406	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
2407	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
2408	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
2409	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
2410	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
2411	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
2412	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
2413	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
2414	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
2415	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
2416	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
2417	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
2418	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
2419	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
2420	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
2421	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
2422	\end{definition}
2423	
2424	\begin{definition}
2425	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
2426	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2427	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
2428	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2429	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
2430	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
2431	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
2432	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
2433	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
2434	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
2435	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
2436	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
2437	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
2438	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
2439	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2440	\end{definition}
2441	
2442	\begin{definition}
2443	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
2444	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2445	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}
2446	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
2447	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2448	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2449	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2450	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
2451	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2452	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2453	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2454	  % Consider the rule
2455	  % $$
2456	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2457	  % $$
2458	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2459	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2460	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2461	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2462	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2463	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2464	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2465	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2466	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2467	  % We will denote this sheaf
2468	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
2469	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2470	  % $$
2471	  % \mathcal{F}
2472	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2473	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2474	  % \longrightarrow
2475	  % \mathcal{G}.
2476	  % $$"
2477	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2478	  (tag area 01CM).}
2479	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
2480	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
2481	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
2482	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
2483	  \[
2484	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
2485	      \;:=\;
2486	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
2487	  \]
2488	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
2489	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
2490	  This value module is the slice specialisation
2491	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
2492	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
2493	
2494	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
2495	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
2496	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
2497	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
2498	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
2499	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
2500	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
2501	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
2502	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
2503	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
2504	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
2505	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
2506	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
2507	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
2508	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
2509	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
2510	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
2511	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
2512	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
2513	
2514	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
2515	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
2516	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
2517	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
2518	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
2519	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
2520	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
2521	\end{definition}
2522	
2523	\begin{lemma}
2524	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
2525	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2526	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
2527	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2528	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2529	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2530	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
2531	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2532	  % "Consider the rule
2533	  % $$
2534	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2535	  % $$
2536	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2537	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2538	  % abelian groups."
2539	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2540	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
2541	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
2542	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
2543	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
2544	  is a restriction map
2545	  \[
2546	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
2547	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
2548	  \]
2549	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
2550	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
2551	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
2552	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
2553	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
2554	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
2555	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
2556	  \[
2557	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
2558	  \]
2559	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
2560	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
2561	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
2562	\end{lemma}
2563	
2564	\begin{proof}
2565	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2566	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
2567	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
2568	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
2569	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
2570	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
2571	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
2572	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
2573	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
2574	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
2575	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
2576	\end{proof}
2577	
2578	\begin{definition}
2579	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
2580	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
2581	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}

2604	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)

2613	  % \mathcal{G}.

2616	  (tag area 01CM).}
2617	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
2618	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2619	  \[
2620	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
2621	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
2622	  \]
2623	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
2624	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
2625	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
2626	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
2627	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
2628	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
2629	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.

2632	\begin{proof}
2633	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2634	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
2635	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
2636	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
2637	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
2638	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
2639	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
2640	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
2641	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
2642	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. This
2643	  is the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2644	  specialised to the unit presheaf.

2647	\begin{lemma}
2648	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
2649	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
2650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
2651	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2652	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2653	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2654	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
2655	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2656	  % "Consider the rule
2657	  % $$
2658	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2659	  % $$
2660	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2661	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2662	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2663	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2664	  (tag area 01CM).}
2665	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
2666	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
2667	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2668	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
2669	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
2670	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
2671	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
2672	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2673	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
2674	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
2675	  \[
2676	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
2677	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
2678	  \]
2679	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
2680	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
2681	\end{lemma}
2682	
2683	\begin{proof}
2684	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2685	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
2686	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
2687	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
2688	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
2689	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
2690	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
2691	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
2692	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
2693	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
2694	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
2695	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
2696	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
2697	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
2698	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
2699	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
2700	\end{proof}

2719	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
2720	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of

2725	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2726	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
2727	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes with
2728	  open-immersion restriction --- the slice formula

2730	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)\),
2731	  the restriction-compatibility being the dual analogue of
2732	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} for the internal hom. Under the trivialisation,
2733	  \[

2736	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
2737	  \]
2738	  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\), the canonical iso of the dual of
2739	  a free rank-one module with itself. Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of
2740	  rank one. (Equivalently, this is item~2 of Stacks
2741	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}, tag 01CR: the dual of an invertible

content:
1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean
2	
3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}

1	\chapter{The relative cotangent presheaf}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the relative cotangent \emph{presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of a morphism of schemes \(f \colon X \to S\), starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) already available in Mathlib. The output is a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules whose sections on each open \(V \subseteq X\) are the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\), where \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\) denotes the \emph{presheaf-level} inverse-image functor (left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\)).
5	
6	The downstream role of this chapter is to provide the \emph{forward direction} of the standard smoothness criterion: smoothness of \(f\) implies that on every affine chart \(V \to U\) (with \(V \subseteq X\), \(U \subseteq S\) affine and \(f V \subseteq U\)) the appLE-algebra K\"ahler differential \(\Omega_{\Gamma(X, V)\,/\,\Gamma(S, U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\). This is the Jacobian-criterion direction and is what connects to the dimension hypothesis on the Jacobian: the smoothness of the curve \(C \to \Spec k\) in the Jacobian definition is the special case \(n = 1\), and the relative dimension of \(\Jac(C)\) is read off from the rank via the same forward implication. The bridge between this affine-chart-wise algebra-K\"ahler form and the section module of the relative cotangent presheaf \(\Omega_{X/S}\) was the former content of milestone~M1; the bridge declaration was excised iter-126 (zero in-tree consumers) and replaced in this chapter by the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities of Section~REF, the off-loop Mathlib-PR work of which proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}. The converse implication of the forward smoothness criterion is \emph{mathematically false} as stated and is documented separately in Section~REF.
7	
8	\section{The relative cotangent presheaf}
9	
10	\begin{definition}
11	
12	\leanok
13	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
14	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
15	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
16	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes. The presheaf-level inverse-image presheaf of rings \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S}\) on \(X\) (the left Kan extension of \(\struct{S}\) along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\), given on each open \(V \subseteq X\) by the colimit \(\mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\)) is paired with \(\struct{X}\) by the canonical adjunction-transpose \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S} \to \struct{X}\) of the comorphism \(f^{\sharp}\colon\struct{S}\to f_{*}\struct{X}\). Applying the presheaf-of-modules K\"ahler differential construction \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\) to this map of presheaves of commutative rings produces a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules on \(X\), the \emph{relative cotangent presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\).
17	\end{definition}
18	
19	\begin{lemma}
20	
21	\leanok
22	[Sections of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on an open]
23	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
24	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler}
25	  
26	  For every open \(V \subseteq X\), the sections of the relative cotangent presheaf are, by construction, the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\):
27	  \[
28	    \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{\struct{X}(V) \,/\, (f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)}.
29	  \]
30	  Here \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) = \mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\) is the presheaf-level inverse-image section ring, \emph{not} \(\struct{S}(U)\) for an arbitrary affine open \(U \supseteq f V\). The relationship between this colimit-source K\"ahler module and the classical ring-theoretic \(\Omega_{B/A}\) for \(A = \struct{S}(U)\), \(B = \struct{X}(V)\) on an affine chart \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}U\) with \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) affine is the content of the bridge documented in Section~REF.
31	\end{lemma}
32	
33	\begin{proof}
34	  
35	  \leanok
36	  By definition of \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\): the construction is built object-wise to be the Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module of the ring map at each open. The identification is therefore by \texttt{rfl} after unfolding the definition. The colimit description of \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\) is the definition of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} as the left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\) in Mathlib.
37	\end{proof}
38	
39	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level \(\Omega_{B/A}\)]
40	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
41	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) together with the universal derivation \(\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}\). The construction of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on each open is, by definition, this Mathlib module applied at the ring level of the presheaf, so all ring-level Mathlib lemmas about \(\Omega_{B/A}\) transfer verbatim under Lemma~REF — but for the \emph{colimit-source} ring \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\), not for \(\struct{S}(U)\). Transferring to the classical \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) source on an affine chart is the bridge of Section~REF.
42	\end{remark}
43	
44	\section{Smoothness criterion for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (forward direction)}
45	
46	The forward direction of the textbook smoothness criterion identifies a necessary condition on the relative K\"ahler differentials: every point of \(X\) admits an affine chart \(V \to U\) over which the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is free of the prescribed rank, with \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) and \(B = \struct{X}(V)\). This is the global version of the algebraic-side fact that an \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~algebra has a free K\"ahler differential module of rank \(n\). We state and prove the theorem directly on the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module, sidestepping the presheaf bridge of Section~REF. The converse implication (a local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) alone implying smoothness) is \emph{false}; see Section~REF.
47	
48	\begin{theorem}\leanok
49	[Smooth implies \(\Omega\) locally free (algebra-K\"ahler form)]
50	  \label{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_locally_free_omega}
52	  
53	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). If \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\), then for every point \(x \in X\) there exist an affine open \(U \subseteq S\), an affine open \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\), such that the K\"ahler differential module
54	  \[
55	    \Omega_{\Gamma(X, V) \,/\, \Gamma(S, U)}
56	  \]
57	  (over the appLE algebra structure \(\Gamma(S, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\) induced by \(f\)) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\).
58	\end{theorem}
59	
60	\begin{proof}
61	  
62	  \leanok
63	  Fix \(x \in X\). We use the abbreviation \(A := \Gamma(S, U)\) and \(B := \Gamma(X, V)\) for the section rings on the affine chart we will produce; the algebra map \(A \to B\) is the appLE map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\) induced by \(f\).
64	
65	  \emph{Step 1: extract a standard-smooth chart at \(x\).} The auto-generated \texttt{mk\_iff} bridge
66	  \[
67	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} \quad [\text{verified}]
68	  \]
69	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth}; produced by the \texttt{@[mk\_iff]} attribute on the class \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}) unfolds the hypothesis \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(f\) into: for every \(x \in X\), there exist affine opens \(U \subseteq S\) and \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \leq f^{-1}U\), such that the induced algebra map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\colon A \to B\) satisfies \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
70	
71	  \emph{Step 2: pass to plain standard-smooth.} The lemma
72	  \[
73	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} \quad [\text{verified}]
74	  \]
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth}) yields \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\) from the relative-dimension-tagged form, unlocking the cotangent-module machinery in the next two steps.
76	
77	  \emph{Step 3: free K\"ahler module on the chart.} The Mathlib instance
78	  \[
79	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
80	  \]
81	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}) synthesises \texttt{Module.Free}~\(B\)~\(\Omega_{B/A}\) from \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\).
82	
83	  \emph{Step 4: pin the rank.} The theorem
84	  \[
85	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
86	  \]
87	  (same file as Step 3) gives \(\rank_B\,\Omega_{B/A} = n\) under the hypotheses \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
88	
89	  \emph{Step 4.5: discharge the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) side condition.} The chart witness \(x \in V\) shows that the open \(V \subseteq X\) is non-empty as a set, so its structure-sheaf section ring \(B = \Gamma(X, V) = \struct{X}(V)\) is non-zero by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (the structure sheaf of a scheme has \texttt{Nontrivial} sections on a non-empty open). This supplies the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) hypothesis required by Step 4.
90	
91	  Combining Steps 1--4.5, the chart \((U, V, e)\) produced by Step 1 satisfies \(x \in V\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,U\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,V\), and under the appLE algebra structure \(A = \Gamma(S, U) \to B = \Gamma(X, V)\), the K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is a free \(B\)-module of rank \(n\). As \(x \in X\) was arbitrary, the statement follows.
92	
93	  This is the forward direction of the Jacobian criterion: Stacks Tag~02G1 (and Hartshorne~II.8, Theorem~II.5.2 in the Noetherian case).
94	
95	  \emph{Mathlib name summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure pieces the proof relies on are
96	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} (Step 1),
97	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} (Step 2),
98	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 3),
99	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 4),
100	  and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (Step 4.5).

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),

10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent

29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier

71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition

75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78

80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}

17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}

22	The bridge consumed by

36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)

52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem

55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of

69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
